text: Hello friends, this video demonstrates laparoscopic repair of duodenal ulcer perforation. Patient was a sixty two years old male who is a smoker presented to the emergency department with a sudden onset of upper abdominal pain of one day duration.
On examination there was severe tenderness over the upper abdomen and the liver dullness was obliterated. X-ray chest was taken and showed free air under diaphragm.

The patient is positioned in a low lithotomy position with a reverse Trendelenburg tilt. A 10mm infraumbilical port is created, and a 30-degree laparoscopic telescope is introduced into the abdominal cavity.
During the initial diagnostic laparoscopy, the surgeon observes a significant amount of fluid collected throughout the peritoneal cavity, consisting of gastric juice and bile.

A 5mm port is placed between the left subcostal margin and the umbilicus at the midclavicular line. The surgeon introduces a suction apparatus to aspirate purulent materials and fluids from the peritoneal cavity, collecting samples for bacteriological study.

The surgeon meticulously examines the pyloroduodenal region to locate the perforation, which is identified as approximately five millimeters in diameter. The size is measured using surgical instruments that are five millimeters wide.

The surgeon performs a thorough peritoneal lavage using three to four liters of normal saline, flushing the peritoneal cavity to clear any residual fluids and debris following the diagnosis of a perforated ulcer.

The BOIS score is used for the evaluation of the severity of the disease. The BOIS score is based on three criterias. One is shock at the time of admission that is systolic blood pressure less than ninety millimeters of mercury and the second one is severe medical illness based on ASA level that is ASA three and four and the third one is delayed presentation that is presentation more than twenty four hours after the symptoms.
The surgeon creates a second working port between the right subcostal margin and the umbilicus at the mid-clavicular line, preparing for further surgical intervention.

If the patient comes less than forty-eight hours after the symptoms, then the fifty percentage of patients will have sterile peritonitis, that is, chemical peritonitis, and these are good candidates for laparoscopic repair. If the presentation is more than forty-eight hours after the initial symptoms, then there will be a chance for dense fibrinous exudates and there is also intestine may plaster to the abdominal wall, which may cause iatrogenic perforation during proctor insertion. And there is also chance for hypercarbia and bad vision due to peritoneal inflammation.
The surgeon examines the pelvis, where a significant amount of purulent fluid is visible. The area is thoroughly cleaned to prevent the development of a pelvic abscess.

The surgeon identifies a readily available omental flap for use in the omental patch. A 10 mm port is placed in the epigastric area, and a liver fan retractor is introduced. The video provides an overview of the different port positions used in the procedure.
A duo polygalactin suture is introduced to the surgical field. The duodenal ulcer is observed to have a healthy border, while the gastric ulcer presents with fibrotic edematous borders.
The surgeon takes biopsy samples from the gastric ulcer, while suture bites are placed 0.5 to 1 centimeter lateral to the healthy margin. The ulcer is closed with an omental patch.
The surgeon carefully performs suturing to repair a perforated peptic ulcer, using the second of two to three separate sutures to ensure secure closure and prevent suture leak, a common complication.

The surgeon assesses the ulcer defect, noting that if the tissue edges are healthy and can be approximated without tension, a primary closure is performed. The defect is then reinforced with an omental patch.

Patient selection for laparoscopic repair is still disputable. There are no firm criteria for patient selection for laparoscopic approach. It is better to avoid laparoscopic repair in patients with septic shock on admission and severe cardiopulmonary comorbidities. Proton term inhibitors and H pylori eradication therapy have significantly decreased the rate of peptic ulcer disease complications. But perforation of duodenal ulcer is still quite frequent in developing countries.

The surgeon places the third suture to close the perforation in the duodenal ulcer. An omental flap is then brought over the perforation and tied in place, reinforcing the closure. This technique is adapted for laparoscopic repair, which offers better visualization and reduced postoperative complications compared to conventional open surgery.
The surgeon removes the needle after completing the suturing of the ulcer repair. Subsequently, a final wash is performed to cleanse the surgical area.
A surgical drain is placed in the sub-hepatic area after aspirating fluid from the pelvis.
